item.description: An agent is an entity that perceives its environment and acts on it to achieve goals. Stuart Jonathan Russell and Peter Norvig defined this in Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (1995): an agent is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon it through actuators. A thermostat is an agent — it perceives temperature and acts by switching a heater. A person is an agent. A software process that reads files, makes decisions, and writes output is an agent.

A command is a named operation that a user invokes to trigger a defined action. The user supplies the name, optionally with arguments, and the system executes the corresponding operation. The command is the basic unit of interaction in any system where a human directs a machine by naming what they want done rather than doing it directly.

The concept originates with the Multics and Unix operating systems of the 1960s and 1970s. Kenneth Lane Thompson and Dennis MacAlistair Ritchie designed the Unix shell as a command interpreter: the user types a name (ls, cd, grep), the shell looks up the corresponding program, and executes it. Each command is a standalone program with a defined interface — it reads input, does one thing, and produces output. Douglas Stuart McIlroy’s Unix philosophy (1978) codified this: “Write programs that do one thing and do it well.” The command is the atom of that philosophy.

A hook is a user-defined action that fires automatically when a specific system event occurs. The system defines the events. The user defines what happens when they fire. This separation — the system controls when, the user controls what — is what makes hooks a mechanism for extension without modification.

The pattern originates in operating systems. Unix signals (Dennis MacAlistair Ritchie and Kenneth Lane Thompson, 1971) allowed a process to register a handler for a system event like SIGTERM or SIGINT. The operating system raises the signal; the process’s registered handler runs. The Emacs text editor (Richard Matthew Stallman, 1976) generalized this into named hook variables: before-save-hook, after-init-hook, and hundreds of others. Each hook is a list of functions. When the event occurs, Emacs calls every function on the list. Users customize Emacs by adding functions to hooks rather than modifying Emacs itself.

An instinct is a behavioral pattern learned from repeated observation that has not yet been formalized into a rule or skill. It occupies the space between raw experience and codified knowledge — a pattern the system has noticed but not yet validated, named, or made permanent.

The biological sense is older. Charles Robert Darwin used “instinct” in On the Origin of Species (1859) for complex behaviors that organisms perform without learning — the honeybee’s comb-building, the cuckoo’s brood parasitism. Nikolaas Tinbergen formalized the study in The Study of Instinct (1951), defining instinct as a fixed action pattern: a stereotyped behavioral sequence triggered by a specific stimulus (the “sign stimulus” or “releaser”), performed to completion once triggered, and present in all members of a species without individual learning. The key insight was that instincts are not learned but inherited — they are the species’ accumulated response to recurring environmental pressures, encoded in genetics rather than individual experience.

Memory is persisted knowledge that survives the boundary of the interaction that produced it. A session’s context exists only while the session is active. Memory is what remains after the session ends — the residue of experience that informs future action.

The psychological taxonomy comes from Endel Tulving’s distinction in Elements of Episodic Memory (1983) between episodic memory (memory of specific events — what happened, when, where) and semantic memory (memory of general knowledge — facts, concepts, relationships independent of when they were learned). Larry Ryan Squire extended this in Memory and Brain (1987) with procedural memory — memory of how to do things, encoded in motor patterns and habits rather than conscious recall. A cyclist’s ability to balance is procedural memory; knowing that bicycles have two wheels is semantic memory; remembering the first time they rode without training wheels is episodic memory.

A prompt is an input that directs an agent’s behavior by specifying what to do, how to do it, or what constraints to observe. In a language-based system, the prompt is the primary interface between a principal and an agent — it is how the principal communicates intent.

The word comes from theater. A prompter sits offstage and feeds lines to actors who have forgotten them. The prompter does not perform; the prompter tells the performer what to say next. The theatrical sense carries into computing: a shell prompt ($, >) tells the user the system is waiting for input. The command prompt is not itself a command — it is the invitation to give one.

A rule is a standing instruction that applies automatically whenever its conditions are met. No one invokes a rule. It is always in effect. When the situation matches, the rule governs what happens.

The concept spans every domain that has governance. In law, a rule is a norm that binds conduct: “No vehicle shall exceed 65 miles per hour on this highway.” The rule applies to every vehicle at all times — no one needs to invoke it. H.L.A. Hart, in The Concept of Law (1961), distinguished primary rules (which govern conduct directly) from secondary rules (which govern how primary rules are created, changed, and adjudicated). The rule of recognition — the secondary rule that identifies which primary rules are valid — is the foundation of a legal system.

A session is a bounded interval of interaction between a user and a system. It has a beginning (the user initiates contact), an end (the user disconnects or the system’s resources expire), and state that persists within the session but does not automatically survive its boundary. When a session ends, its state is lost unless something explicitly saves it.

The concept comes from timesharing systems. In Multics (Fernando José Corbató, 1965), multiple users shared a single computer by taking turns. Each user’s session began when they logged in and ended when they logged out. The operating system maintained each user’s state — open files, running processes, environment variables — for the duration of the session and cleaned it up afterward. The session was the unit of resource allocation: the system gave each session a share of processor time, memory, and storage.

A skill is a reusable body of knowledge that an agent loads on demand to perform a specific kind of work. A tool extends what an agent can do. A skill shapes how the agent does it. A hammer lets you drive nails; knowing carpentry tells you where to put them.

The distinction between tool and skill runs through every domain that studies competence. Aristotle distinguished technē (the knowledge of how to produce something) from the instruments used in production. A surgeon’s scalpel is a tool; the surgeon’s knowledge of anatomy, trained judgment about when to cut, and practiced hand are skills. Gilbert Ryle, in The Concept of Mind (1949), formalized this as the difference between “knowing that” (propositional knowledge) and “knowing how” (practical competence). A skill is knowing how — it cannot be fully captured in a set of propositions, though propositions can guide its development.

Plan

Formal definition

A Plan is a pair (O,≺)(\mathcal{O}, \prec):

(O:FinSet(Operation),  ≺⊆O×O:StrictPartialOrder)(\mathcal{O} : \mathrm{FinSet}(\mathrm{Operation}),\; \prec \subseteq \mathcal{O} \times \mathcal{O} : \mathrm{StrictPartialOrder})

together with:

  • A precondition pre:State→Bool\mathrm{pre} : \mathrm{State} \to \mathrm{Bool} specifying the states from which the plan applies
  • A goal condition goal:State→Bool\mathrm{goal} : \mathrm{State} \to \mathrm{Bool} specifying the states the plan aims to achieve

where:

  • O\mathcal{O} is the operation set — the finite collection of steps (intended actions, operations, or tasks) that the plan consists of; each step o∈Oo \in \mathcal{O} has its own preconditions (what must hold before oo executes) and effects (what oo changes in the state when it executes)
  • ≺\prec is the ordering constraint — a strict partial order on O\mathcal{O} specifying which steps must precede which; o1≺o2o_1 \prec o_2 means “step o1o_1 must be completed before step o2o_2 begins”; ≺\prec is the plan’s causal and temporal skeleton; steps not related by ≺\prec may execute in any order (or concurrently)

The plan (O,≺)(\mathcal{O}, \prec) is valid iff: there exists a total ordering o1,o2,…,ono_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n of O\mathcal{O} extending ≺\prec such that, starting from any state satisfying pre\mathrm{pre}, sequentially executing o1,…,ono_1, \ldots, o_n produces a state satisfying goal\mathrm{goal}.

Markdown Frontmatter

YAML frontmatter in markdown files is a serialization of an RDF directed labeled graph.

The RDF data model (W3C 2004; Cyganiak, Wood, Lanthaler 2014) represents knowledge as a directed labeled graph G = (V, E) where:

  • V is a set of nodes — IRIs (named resources) or literals (typed values)
  • E is a set of directed labeled edges — triples (subject, predicate, object) where subject ∈ IRIs, predicate ∈ IRIs, object ∈ IRIs ∪ Literals

A YAML frontmatter block is a serialization of the RDF triples for one subject node. Each .md file is a subject IRI; each frontmatter key is a predicate IRI; each frontmatter value is the object — an IRI if it references a known entity id, a typed literal otherwise. The complete set of all frontmatter blocks in the corpus is a serialization of the full RDF graph over the corpus.
